sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgical resection is the only potentially curative technique for managing pancreatic cancer. The goals of surgery include an oncologic resection of the primary tumor and regional lymph nodes. Careful intraoperative staging should rule out peritoneal, liver, and distant lymph node metastases, and resection should only be done in the absence of distant disease [PANC-E 1 of 3, MS-16]. Resections should be done at institutions that perform at least 15–20 pancreatic resections annually [PANC-A 1 of 8].

Principles:
- Achievement of an R0 resection is the primary goal, as margin-positive specimens are associated with poor long-term survival [PANC-E 1 of 3, references 1,2].
- Consider frozen section analysis of the pancreatic neck and bile duct. Avoid cautery at margins sent for frozen section. If tumor located within 5 mm of margins, consider further excision to ensure at least 5 mm of clearance [PANC-E 1 of 3].
- Medial dissection of pancreatic head lesions is best achieved by complete mobilization of PV and SMV from uncinate process. Skeletalization of lateral, posterior, and anterior borders of SMA down to adventitia maximizes uncinate yield and radial margin [PANC-E 1 of 3, references 3,4].
- Differentiation of tumor infiltration into vein wall from tumor-related desmoplasia is frequently impossible. Liberal use of partial or complete vein excision when tumor infiltration is suspected is supported by data [PANC-E 1 of 3].
- Positive cytology from washings obtained at laparoscopy or laparotomy is equivalent to M1 disease [PANC-A 2 of 8].
- Spleen preservation is not indicated in adenocarcinoma [PANC-E 2 of 3].
- For pancreas neck adenocarcomas, depending on extent of involvement, extended pancreaticoduodenectomy, extended distal pancreatectomy, or total pancreatectomy may be required [PANC-E 2 of 3, references 10,11].
- There is a potential benefit of re-resection for recurrences in selected patients after careful multidisciplinary evaluation with detailed restaging assessment and multimodal therapy care plan [PANC-6A footnote ii, PANC-E 2 of 3].

cards.name: Pancreatoduodenectomy (Whipple Procedure)
cards.detail:
Approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic or robotic)
Indications: Adenocarcinomas of the pancreatic head and uncinate process [PANC-E 1 of 3].
Key complications: Pancreatic fistula (19.8% for pancreaticojejunostomy vs 8.0% for pancreaticogastrostomy in one trial [PANC-E 2 of 3, reference 243]), delayed gastric emptying, bile leak, hemorrhage, infection, wound complications. Pancreaticogastrostomy may be associated with lower risk of fistula formation (RR, 0.41; 95% CI, 0.21–0.62) [PANC-E 2 of 3, reference 244].
Operative mortality: <5% mortality in experienced centers [MS-16, reference 183]. Mortality is significantly lower at high-volume centers (3.8% at high-volume vs 16.3% at very-low-volume centers [MS-23, reference 277]).

cards.name: Distal Pancreatectomy with En Bloc Splenectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Open or minimally invasive (laparoscopic preferred for distal pancreatectomy; meta-analysis showed decreased intraoperative blood loss, time to first oral intake, and length of hospital stay [MS-20, reference 225])
Indications: Adenocarcinomas of the pancreatic body and tail [PANC-E 2 of 3].
Key complications: Pancreatic fistula, bleeding, infection, splenectomy-related complications. An R0 resection mandates en bloc organ removal beyond spleen alone in up to 40% of patients [PANC-E 2 of 3, references 7,8].
Operative mortality: Data from meta-analyses show favorable perioperative outcomes with laparoscopic approach [MS-20, reference 225].

cards.name: Total Pancreatectomy
cards.detail:
Approach: Open
Indications: May be required when tumor diffusely involves the pancreas, is present at multiple sites, or for pancreas neck adenocarcinomas to achieve R0 resection [MS-19, PANC-E 2 of 3].
Key complications: Pancreatic exocrine and endocrine insufficiency requiring lifelong enzyme replacement and insulin management.
Operative mortality: Variable; performed at experienced centers.

cards.name: Staging Laparoscopy
cards.detail:
Approach: Minimally invasive
Indications: Diagnostic staging to rule out metastases not detected on imaging, especially for body and tail lesions, or in patients with high-risk features [PANC-A 2 of 8]. Can be performed at time of original diagnosis and following neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
Key complications: Low complication profile. Concerns about trocar-site recurrences are not well supported by clinical data [PANC-A 2 of 8, reference 86].
Operative mortality: Very low.

cards.name: Biliary-Enteric Bypass (Open)
cards.detail:
Approach: Open
Indications: For patients found to have unresectable disease at surgery who present with jaundice. Provides durable palliation [PANC-8, MS-48].
Key complications: May be combined with gastrojejunostomy and celiac plexus neurolysis.
Operative mortality: Palliative pro
